reasoning: Mexico arrive on an eight-game unbeaten run in 2026, having beaten Serbia 5-1 and conceded only twice across the entire year, with a settled defensive partnership and dangerous set-piece threat via Montes and Vásquez. The Estadio Azteca altitude (~2,240m) is a genuine physical tax on South Africa, who are a sea-level team returning to the World Cup after a 16-year absence and whose pre-tournament warmups produced only a draw with Nicaragua and a draw with Jamaica.

South Korea hold a modest edge rooted in superior warm-up form (6-0 over two friendlies vs Czech's 5-2), stronger top-end squad quality around Son Heung-min and Kim Min-jae, and a meaningful altitude advantage at Guadalajara (~1,566m) where Czech Republic, based in sea-level Dallas, face acclimatisation costs. Czech defensive fragility was exposed even against non-qualifier Guatemala, and their 20-year World Cup absence adds pressure, though Schick and Soucek make them a genuine set-piece threat. The match remains close — a draw is nearly as likely as a Korea win — and the 1-0 scoreline reflects a tight, low-scoring opener where a single moment of Son or Lee Kang-in quality tips the balance.

Canada enter as the stronger side on paper (FIFA rank ~31 vs. Bosnia's 65) with home-crowd backing at a sold-out BMO Field where they have lost just once in 28 matches, and key attackers Jonathan David and Cyle Larin fully fit; however, captain Alphonso Davies is officially ruled out and Moïse Bombito is also absent, stripping the back-line and left flank of real quality.

USA hold a meaningful edge at home at SoFi with the crowd, a superior FIFA ranking, and a recent 2-1 friendly win over this same Paraguay side. The likely absence of Julio Enciso — Paraguay's most dangerous attacker — blunts their already limited creativity (fewest CONMEBOL qualifying goals), while Chris Richards returning to anchor the US backline is a further boost. However, Paraguay's ultra-defensive 4-2-3-1 under Alfaro makes them hard to break down, World Cup group openers frequently end level, and USA's pre-tournament defensive leakiness (conceding 4 in the two friendlies without Richards) warrants caution on backing a comfortable home win.

Switzerland hold a commanding edge across every factor: a 37-place FIFA ranking gap, a settled top-club core (Akanji, Xhaka, Freuler, Embolo, Kobel) with no injury concerns, and an unbeaten qualifying run conceding just two goals. Qatar arrive in dire form — winless in their last five, scoreless over 373 minutes, with key March friendlies cancelled and a squad almost entirely drawn from the Qatari domestic league. The venue is neutral and at sea level, removing any thermal/altitude equaliser.

Brazil hold a genuine talent edge with Vinicius Jr., Raphinha and Alisson, but the losses of Rodrygo, Estêvão and Militão meaningfully thin their attacking and defensive depth, and Neymar's fitness at 34 is uncertain. Morocco arrive as AFCON champions, unbeaten in 29 matches, with an elite defensive structure and Hakimi fit after his Champions League triumph — though they too are weakened by the last-minute losses of Aguerd and Ezzalzouli.

Scotland arrive in dominant form — back-to-back 4-0 wins over Curacao and Bolivia — with Ché Adams, McTominay and Shankland all firing, and hold a 45-place FIFA ranking advantage over Haiti. However, Scotland are missing five first-choice players (Gilmour, Hickey, Doak, Souttar, Christie), which hollows out both their best XI and bench depth. Haiti showed they can be dangerous on the counter with pace, and their motivation to take three points from their most winnable group game is acute. The draw probability is nudged above the market line to reflect Scotland's injury-hit squad and Haiti's potential to nick something via transition play, but Scotland's class should ultimately prevail.

Türkiye hold a clear edge in squad quality — a rated attack of 79 vs Australia's 67, with Güler fit and Çalhanoğlu available — and arrive in better recent form (W-W-D-L-W vs Australia's W-D-L-W-D). Australia's injury list is meaningful: the absences of first-choice midfielder McGree and wing-back Miller disrupt Popovic's preferred XI, while Souttar returns from 18 months out and is match-rusty. Vancouver's sea-level, neutral venue cancels any climate edge, and both sides face equivalent travel.

Germany enter on a nine-match winning run with an elite attacking trident of Wirtz, Musiala and Havertz fully fit, and Neuer confirmed available after a calf scare. Curaçao (FIFA 82nd) are World Cup debutants whose preparation included a 4-1 loss to Scotland; their plan will be a deep low block, but the quality and depth gap is enormous. Houston is sea-level and neutral to both sides, offering Germany no venue penalty.

Netherlands hold clear advantages in squad depth and FIFA ranking (7th vs 19th), but arrive with significant absences — Xavi Simons (ACL), Jurrien Timber (groin, tournament out), and de Ligt — blunting their creativity and defensive options. Japan enter in exceptional form with six consecutive friendly wins, including a historic 1-0 win over England, and retain Endo, Kubo, and Kamada despite losing Mitoma and Minamino. The AT&T Stadium's indoor, sea-level setting neutralises any environmental edge.

Ecuador arrive in excellent shape — a 19-match unbeaten run, the meanest defence in CONMEBOL qualifying (5 goals conceded in 18 games), and elite spine in Caicedo, Pacho, and Hincapié all fit — making them rightful favourites despite being the nominal away side. Ivory Coast are dangerous on the counter with Amad Diallo and Simon Adingra, and their morale is sky-high after beating France 2-1 in their final tune-up, but they lack a clinical No.9 (Haller omitted) and have historically struggled in the World Cup group stage.

Sweden hold a clear squad quality edge, with Gyökeres and Isak forming one of the tournament's most dangerous strike partnerships, and their play-off wins over Ukraine and Poland showed they can score freely in competitive pressure matches. Tunisia's warm-up form is genuinely alarming — a 5-0 loss to Belgium (admittedly with 10 men for 30 minutes) and a 1-0 defeat to Austria — raising real questions about their defensive readiness against European pace and pressing. However, Tunisia's flawless CAF qualifying record (10 clean sheets in 10) shows their defensive structure is real in competitive contexts, and MD1 opener caution means a tighter match is plausible.

Spain are reigning European champions with a world-class squad built around Yamal, Pedri, Rodri and Nico Williams, entered the tournament unbeaten in their last 10+ matches and are heavy favourites in every metric; their only notable injury absentee is Fermín López (fractured metatarsal), while Yamal and Nico Williams were rested as a precaution in the final warm-up but are expected fit for MD1. Cape Verde are first-time World Cup participants ranked 69th, lack individual star power or a world-class striker, and face a massive quality gap; their pre-tournament form (3-0 vs Bermuda, 3-0 vs Serbia) is encouraging but the level of opposition is incomparable.

Belgium enter on a 13-game unbeaten run, including a 5-0 warm-up rout of Tunisia, and boast clear squad superiority through De Bruyne, Doku, and Tielemans. However, Lukaku's severe lack of match fitness and De Bruyne's recent return from surgery introduce real attacking bluntness, while Courtois remains a doubt. Egypt arrive with an elite counter-attacking pairing in a less-than-fully-fit Salah and a sharp Marmoush, backed by a defence that conceded only twice in 10 qualifying matches — a setup tailor-made to frustrate and punish on the break.

Uruguay hold a clear squad-quality edge with Valverde, Ugarte and a La Liga-calibre defence, and the market's ~63% away probability is broadly justified. However, two significant factors compress the gap: Saudi Arabia arrive with genuine upset pedigree (Argentina 2022) and a new coach Donis who favours defensive solidity, while Uruguay enter the tournament with a chronic goalscoring crisis — just 2 goals in their last 360 minutes — and Ronald Araújo almost certainly absent through injury for this opener. Both sides have won just 1 of their last 5 matches, and Bielsa's high-press 4-3-3 versus a disciplined Saudi low-block is a recipe for a tight, low-scoring game.

Iran (FIFA ~20th) carry a significant quality edge over New Zealand (85th), with Taremi leading a settled, experienced attack and a defensively solid qualifying record of 11 wins in 16 games. New Zealand will sit deep — as they did in 2010 when they drew all three group games — but a 4-0 pre-tournament friendly loss to Haiti raises real questions about their ceiling against better opposition, and defender Pijnaker is a fitness concern. Iran's off-pitch disruptions (Tijuana base camp, visa saga, Azmoun's political exclusion) introduce marginal uncertainty, nudging the win probability only slightly above the book's 49.4%.

France possess a generational talent advantage — Mbappe, Dembele, Olise and Doue give Deschamps one of the most dangerous attacks in the tournament, and their pre-tournament wins over Brazil and Colombia confirm elite-level readiness. Senegal, however, are no pushover: Mane leads a disciplined, physically imposing side that has historically troubled France (2-0 all-time H2H), and their counter-attacking pace via Sarr and Mane can punish a French defence that conceded in both warm-up games.

Norway arrive with a fully fit squad including Haaland and a recently recovered Ødegaard, a perfect 8-win qualifying campaign, and superior quality at every position. Iraq's entire 26-man squad is available and their defensive shape under Arnold is disciplined, but the talent gap is enormous — Norway are 26 FIFA ranking places higher with a frontline of Haaland, Sørloth, and Nusa that Iraq have no answer for. The market's ~76% Norway win probability aligns tightly with the form, availability, and style-matchup evidence; a slight upward nudge to 77% is warranted given Ødegaard's confirmed fitness.

Argentina are heavy favourites as defending world champions with a deep squad featuring Lautaro Martínez, Mac Allister, Romero and Di María-era depth, though Messi is managing a left hamstring strain and did not feature in the 2-0 win over Honduras, while Balerdi has been ruled out entirely — if Messi is fit by June 17 this line-up is elite, if not it still outclasses Algeria by a wide margin.

Austria hold a decisive squad quality and tactical edge — a FIFA top-25 side with top Bundesliga talent, led by Rangnick's high-pressing 4-2-3-1 — against Jordan's World Cup debutants whose spine is largely domestic-league based. The loss of Baumgartner (thigh, warm-up injury) trims Austria's midfield quality, while Jordan are also missing their top scorer Alnemat (ACL), partially offset by Olwan's recovery. Jordan's compact 5-2-3 defensive block and Al-Tamari-led counters give them a credible upset path, nudging the draw probability slightly above the book line, but Austria's superior depth, organization, and high stakes motivation make a comfortable win the most likely outcome.

Portugal enter as reigning Nations League champions ranked 5th globally, with an elite midfield quartet of Fernandes, Bernardo Silva, Vitinha and João Neves and unbeaten in 12 of their last 13 matches including a 2-0 win over the USA and 2-1 over Chile; no significant injury absentees confirmed. DR Congo return to the World Cup for the first time in 52 years with a diaspora-built squad ranked 46th — Wissa's pace and Mbemba's defensive leadership are real threats but the squad quality gap is vast.

Ghana hold a clear squad-quality edge on paper — Semenyo, Iñaki Williams, Partey and Ayew outclass Panama's roster in individual talent — but their availability crisis (Kudus, Salisu, Djiku all absent; Partey carrying a groin concern) and dire recent form (one draw, five losses since October 2025) substantially erode that advantage. Panama arrive better-prepared, having played Brazil, Bosnia and the Dominican Republic in warm-ups, while Ghana's final tune-up vs Honduras was cancelled; Panama's low-block style at BMO Field (sea-level Toronto, no venue edge for either side) will make this tight and hard to break down.

Colombia carry a decisive squad quality edge — Luis Díaz (26 goals/23 assists at Bayern), James Rodríguez, and a settled Néstor Lorenzo system that went 28 games unbeaten and reached the 2024 Copa América final — against a World Cup debutant side whose pre-tournament form is patchy (lost 2-0 to Canada, trailed Netherlands 1-0 at 66' on June 8). The Azteca's 2,250m altitude is a genuine equaliser that rewards compact defending and counters, marginally inflating the draw probability versus sea level, but neither side is particularly altitude-adapted so the edge is mild.

England hold a clear squad-quality edge — ranked 4th globally with Kane, Saka, Bellingham, and Rice all available — but come in with an unsettled system, notable absentees (Foden, Palmer, TAA), and a modest pre-tournament record including friendly losses to Senegal and Japan. Croatia are formidable tournament opponents with a historically over-performing mentality, though key players Modrić (cheekbone, game-day doubt) and Gvardiol (returning from five months out) are less than fully sharp. The indoor AT&T Stadium neutralises heat/altitude factors for both sides.

Czechia hold a meaningful squad-quality edge and enter with a full complement of available players, while South Africa are severely weakened by the suspensions of both Sithole and Zwane (red cards vs. Mexico), losing key defensive and creative personnel simultaneously. Mokoena carries a yellow card, adding further pressure on Bafana's already-depleted midfield, and South Africa failed to score or seriously threaten vs. Mexico even with 11 men. Atlanta is a sea-level, neutral venue that provides no altitude edge for either side, and the 7-day rest gap is equal.

Switzerland are the clearly superior side on squad depth and tournament pedigree, but their MD1 performance (dominant yet conceding a 94th-minute own goal vs Qatar) reveals a tendency to sit back and invite pressure. Bosnia are a disciplined, low-block side with genuine set-piece threat who took the lead against Canada — they are no pushover. The book's 60.7% implied Swiss win probability looks slightly generous given Switzerland's unconvincing form (one win in their last four pre-tournament matches, a 4-3 loss to Germany) and Bosnia's organization; I trim Switzerland fractionally and redistribute toward draw and Bosnia.

Canada are the clear quality side at home in Vancouver with Jonathan David, Cyle Larin and a potentially returning Alphonso Davies all available, and the partisan BC Place crowd provides a significant intangible edge; the book's 73% home-win implied probability is broadly sound but nudged down slightly given Canada's recent clinical struggles — they dominated Bosnia yet needed a late Larin substitute appearance to salvage a draw, and Davies remains a fitness doubt.

Mexico hold a meaningful venue edge at Estadio Akron (1,567m altitude, hostile crowd), both teams come in on three points and full confidence after MD1 wins, but Mexico absorb the suspension of Montes while South Korea carry minor injury doubts around Lee Kang-In and Bae Jun-Ho. South Korea's 3-4-2-1 with Son, Lee Kang-In and Hwang In-beom is a genuine counter-attacking threat that can exploit Mexico's transitional moments, compressing the gap between the sides.
